title: Willow fruit bowl
subtitle: Open round willow bowl for the kitchen table
excerpt: A round willow fruit bowl: 30 cm across and 12 cm deep, worked on a round base with a curved slewing weave that sets the bowl's shape. Open enough to see the fruit; firm enough to hold a full load. An intermediate project for a maker who has completed a basic round basket.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for basket bowl forms; Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) references willow kitchen bowls.

glossaryTerms.definition: Basketry weave in which two or more rods are woven together as one, for speed and to add body to the weave. Slewing gives the bowl sides their curve.
Willow basketry weave in which a single rod is woven over and under alternate stakes.
A tight basketry weave using three or more rods together, strengthening the base-to-side transition.
The first few rows of waling worked after staking-up, locking the stakes into their upright position.
Inserting the upright stakes alongside the base spokes to form the sides of the basket.

content.text: A fruit bowl differs from a round shopping or log basket in one key respect: the sides must curve outward, not rise vertically. The flare is achieved by
slewing
the sides at an angle; the stakes lean outward, and the slewing weave works around them at that lean. The base-to-side transition is set by the
upsett
waling; the first 3 rows are worked with the stakes slightly outward, establishing the bowl shape before the slewing takes over.
Materials
Brown or buff willow, 2-foot and 3-foot lengths. Base sticks in 3-foot lengths (heavier grade). Approximately 600 g of willow in total for a 30 cm bowl.
Base and staking-up
Work a standard round base: 6 base sticks, 6 worked through a slath of 6, tied off and paired to 15 cm diameter.
Staking up
follows: insert two stakes beside each base spoke (24 stakes in total). Work the
upsett
waling with the stakes at 45 degrees outward rather than vertical: 3 rows of three-rod
waling
. This sets the outward lean of the bowl sides.
The sides

Work the main body in two-rod slewing: two rods worked together as one, each pair woven over and under alternate stakes. The two-rod slewing fills the weave faster than single randing and the thicker strand gives the bowl wall more body. Continue to 12 cm above the base.
Border

Finish with 3 rows of three-rod waling at the rim; then a simple 3-rod border: take each stake, bend it over the top two rows, and tuck the tip behind the second stake to the right. Trim ends inside the bowl.
Variations
Deeper bowl: work 18 to 20 cm of slewing for a deeper form that will hold a bread loaf.
Decorative stripe: single-rod
randing
in a contrasting willow colour between every 4 rows of slewing.
Care
Do not place the bowl where it will hold water; the base sticks can soften and the bowl will lose its shape. Wipe clean with a damp cloth.

items.symptom: Bowl sides rise vertically instead of flaring outward
items.cause: Upsett waling worked with stakes vertical rather than at an outward angle
items.fix: In the next session, hold the stakes at 45 degrees outward while working the first 3 rows of upsett. Use a rubber band around the basket at rim height to keep the stakes at the correct angle while the upsett sets.
